title: Alex Tastes Death And Finds A Nice Restaurant

jokes.text: What kind of heel you think I am?
jokes.setup: Asked if he cares about shot employee while continuing to dispatch cabs
jokes.punchline: Louie acts offended at suggestion he doesn't care while demonstrating complete lack of empathy
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie self-delusion - asking what kind of heel they think he is while being exactly that kind of heel. Character-specific irony.

jokes.text: You're a sick person.
jokes.setup: Louie's complete emotional detachment about Alex being shot
jokes.punchline: Understated assessment of Louie's obvious psychological issues
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - the massive understatement of calling Louie's sociopathic behavior just 'sick' lands well.

jokes.text: What makes you think it was a Jew? / Oh, uh, just a hunch.
jokes.setup: Alex describing the robbery, listeners assume he's about to identify the robber
jokes.punchline: Bobby asks completely random question about robber being Jewish with Alex giving casual response
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - comes out of nowhere and subverts expectation completely. Bobby's random question and Alex's matter-of-fact response create absurdist moment.

jokes.text: Oh, thanks. I got them on sale. You like them?
jokes.setup: Someone telling Alex to take time off after traumatic experience
jokes.punchline: Alex mishears and thinks they're complimenting his clothes
jokes.explanation: Another hearing loss gag that works because it shows Alex's complete obliviousness to the serious conversation happening around him.

jokes.text: Yeah, but I don't know if the same old saying applies to getting shot off a horse.
jokes.setup: Alex using 'get back on the horse' metaphor for returning to work after trauma
jokes.punchline: Literal interpretation combining the metaphor with the actual shooting incident
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that takes a common metaphor and literalizes it in context. Well-structured with good economy of language.

jokes.text: Hey, Louie, I got a tattoo. / Oh, yeah, and losers.
jokes.setup: Louie ranting that people with tattoos are 'morons, maniacs and lowlifes'
jokes.punchline: Tony reveals he has tattoo, Louie adds 'losers' to his list without missing beat
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and character work. Louie's instant addition to his insult list shows both his quick wit and complete lack of shame about insulting Tony directly.

jokes.text: Sorry, off duty. I'll pick up the next one.
jokes.setup: Alex claims he's fine and ready to work normally
jokes.punchline: Immediately avoids picking up a passenger
jokes.explanation: Shows Alex's fear despite his brave words - actions contradict his claims of being fine

jokes.text: Next one that doesn't have a safety pin through his nose.
jokes.setup: Alex claiming he's not nervous, just being selective about passengers
jokes.punchline: Very specific and paranoid criteria for avoiding passengers
jokes.explanation: Shows Alex's growing paranoia through specific visual detail. The specificity of 'safety pin through nose' makes it funnier than generic 'suspicious person.'

jokes.text: Well, it's not Sneezy.
jokes.setup: Alex testing fake priest's knowledge of 12 Apostles, priest struggling to name final one
jokes.punchline: Priest suggests Disney dwarf name when desperate for 12th Apostle
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment. The desperation leading to mixing biblical apostles with Snow White dwarfs is perfectly executed and highly quotable.

jokes.text: No, I don't think it is Sneezy.
jokes.setup: Fake priest suggesting Sneezy as 12th Apostle
jokes.punchline: Alex's understated confirmation that dwarf is not apostle
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response that extends the absurdity. Alex treating the ridiculous suggestion seriously makes it funnier.

jokes.text: The tongue is mightier than the sword. / Talk like that's a good way to get your tongue cut off.
jokes.setup: Jim advocating peaceful communication over weapons for self-defense
jokes.punchline: Tony's literal and violent interpretation of what happens to peaceful people
jokes.explanation: Dark but clever subversion of the pen/sword saying. Tony's street-smart cynicism cuts through Jim's idealism with violent logic.

jokes.text: What's the matter with you? / I'm having a heart attack. / Well, have it somewheres else. I could've tripped and broke my neck.
jokes.setup: Bobby demonstrating fake heart attack defense strategy
jokes.punchline: Louie's completely unsympathetic response to apparent medical emergency
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - faced with apparent heart attack, his only concern is potential inconvenience to himself. Character-driven and believable.

jokes.text: See? I convinced him. / Yeah. Play some rummy?
jokes.setup: Bobby claiming his heart attack performance was convincing
jokes.punchline: Tony's complete indifference to the 'demonstration' and immediate topic change
jokes.explanation: Tony's casual dismissal of Bobby's 'success' undercuts the moment perfectly. The immediate shift to rummy shows complete disinterest.

jokes.text: You're not used to criticism, are you?
jokes.setup: Alex announcing he's quitting after booking nothing
jokes.punchline: Louie treating life-changing decision as simple sensitivity to workplace feedback
jokes.explanation: Louie's complete inability to understand human emotion, reducing Alex's trauma-based career change to workplace sensitivity. Very character-specific.

jokes.text: Because who cares about money when people's personal feelings are involved?
jokes.setup: Louie admitting Alex quitting will cost him money
jokes.punchline: Louie's obvious sarcasm about caring more about feelings than money
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie sarcasm - saying the opposite of what he believes with complete transparency. Character-appropriate but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Hey, everybody, I'm quitting. / What?! What for? / Well, it's very simple. I'm scared.
jokes.setup: Alex demonstrating how he'll break news to fellow drivers
jokes.punchline: Blunt, honest delivery of embarrassing truth instead of face-saving excuse
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alex's honest nature. The simple, direct admission of fear is both funny and endearing.

jokes.text: You're a lily-livered, yellow-bellied, namby-pamby, mealymouth chicken!
jokes.setup: Louie getting increasingly frustrated with Alex's decision
jokes.punchline: Over-the-top string of old-fashioned cowardice insults
jokes.explanation: Funny escalation of insults but relies on archaic language for humor rather than wit. Character-appropriate for Louie's old-school style.

jokes.text: You're right, Louie.
jokes.setup: Louie's over-the-top string of insults calling Alex a coward
jokes.punchline: Alex calmly agreeing with the harsh assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Louie's tirade. Alex's calm acceptance of being called a coward completely undermines Louie's attempt to shame him.

jokes.text: You could make cheese. / Why would I want to do that? / Somebody has to.
jokes.setup: Alex listing possible career alternatives to cab driving
jokes.punchline: Bobby's random suggestion followed by unexpectedly logical reasoning
jokes.explanation: Absurdist moment that works because Bobby's logic is simultaneously random and undeniable. The economy of the exchange is perfect.

jokes.text: Yeah, somebody else will do that for you.
jokes.setup: Alex describing cab driving as job where you won't kill yourself working
jokes.punchline: Tony's dark reminder about who will do the killing instead
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor that perfectly punctures Alex's positive spin on cab driving. The wordplay on 'killing yourself' is expertly executed.

jokes.text: I mean, a guy leaves the neighborhood, you see him more than when he lived next door.
jokes.setup: Alex trying to reassure friends they'll stay in touch after he quits
jokes.punchline: Common but false social promise delivered with obvious insincerity
jokes.explanation: Relatable observation about hollow social promises. Works because everyone knows it's not true but we all say it anyway.

jokes.text: My car goes fine, why do you ask? / I'm sorry, we don't serve herring, but I can recommend the escargot.
jokes.setup: Customer asking about escargot (French snails)
jokes.punchline: Waiter mishearing as 'S-car-go' and then 'herring'
jokes.explanation: Predictable wordplay joke about mispronouncing fancy French food. Functional but not particularly clever or original.

jokes.text: Dr. Wheeler? / Well, it's easier to get reservations at joints like this if they think you're a doctor.
jokes.setup: Maitre d' addressing Elaine as doctor
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of lying to get better restaurant treatment
jokes.explanation: Relatable social deception that many people do. Elaine's matter-of-fact delivery sells it.

jokes.text: So, uh, how'd that heart job go this morning, Doc?
jokes.setup: Bobby playing along with doctor charade
jokes.punchline: Awkwardly obvious fake question about medical practice
jokes.explanation: Bobby's attempt to help maintain the lie is painfully obvious and awkward, which creates mild cringe humor.

jokes.text: There ain't even no gum stuck to this table.
jokes.setup: Tony expressing amazement at fancy restaurant quality
jokes.punchline: Using absence of gum as marker of high-class establishment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony line - his working-class perspective on what constitutes luxury. Character-specific and endearing.

jokes.text: Will you keep your drawers on, lady? The guy's got six tables. He can't be everywhere at once.
jokes.setup: Impatient customer demanding waiter's attention
jokes.punchline: Tony's crude defense of Alex using inappropriate expression
jokes.explanation: Tony defending Alex but using completely inappropriate language for upscale restaurant creates good cringe comedy.

jokes.text: With cheese.
jokes.setup: Customer asking how potatoes au gratin are prepared
jokes.punchline: Alex's obvious, unhelpful response to fancy food question
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - the complete obviousness of the answer to what was meant as sophisticated culinary inquiry.

jokes.text: However, on the other hand being a waiter, nobody goes like this to me.
jokes.setup: Alex weighing pros and cons of cab driving vs waiting tables
jokes.punchline: References getting shot as casual workplace hazard comparison
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that treats the shooting as mundane workplace consideration. The casual gesture reference makes it brilliantly understated.

jokes.text: Of course, as a cab driver, my time is my own... But of course, you know, as a cab driver, somebody goes like this to me.
jokes.setup: Alex weighing pros and cons of each job
jokes.punchline: References getting shot with gesture
jokes.explanation: Good physical callback to the shooting incident that grounds the career discussion in reality.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, but that's one in a million. Big deal. One time, you know?
jokes.setup: Alex pointing out that waiters don't get shot
jokes.punchline: Bobby minimizing attempted murder as rare inconvenience
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Louie's earlier minimization of the shooting. Bobby adopts the same absurd perspective.

jokes.text: Joker!
jokes.setup: Bobby pointing out Alex has made wonderful friends at restaurant
jokes.punchline: Alex's immediate dismissive reaction to the obvious lie
jokes.explanation: Quick, honest reaction that punctures the pretense about restaurant friendships. Alex's directness is refreshing.

jokes.text: This is very depressing.
jokes.setup: Alex receiving $20 tip, showing how much more money he makes as waiter
jokes.punchline: Tony's understated reaction to seeing their friend's much better financial situation
jokes.explanation: Tony's honest assessment of seeing how much better Alex is doing financially. The understatement makes it funnier than dramatic reaction would.

jokes.text: They love the appetizer.
jokes.setup: Alex just quit his restaurant job and is walking out
jokes.punchline: Oblivious waiter updating Alex on table he just abandoned
jokes.explanation: Mild awkward humor from timing mismatch - waiter giving work update just as Alex quits. More situational than clever.

jokes.text: Aw, what the heck. Might as well take a chance.
jokes.setup: Alex back to cab driving, initially rejecting passenger as 'off duty'
jokes.punchline: Alex deciding to embrace the danger he was previously afraid of
jokes.explanation: Nice character arc completion - Alex going from paranoid to accepting risk. Shows growth and return to his essential nature.

tmdb_overview: After being grazed by a mugger's bullet, Alex decides to give up his cab.
insight: Alex's near-death experience generates steady character comedy at 1.46 jokes per minute.
